headline: Spain's Donkey Brigades Turn Ancient Labor Into Modern Fire Prevention
meta_description: Spanish regions are enlisting donkeys to graze firebreaks and prevent wildfires, a low-tech solution that's proving more effective than sheep or goats.
summary_short: In Spain's Doñana National Park, donkeys have kept wildfires at bay for 9 years by doing what they do best: eating vegetation that could fuel fires.
body:
While Europe's recent heat dome ignited wildfires across Spain, one place remained remarkably safe: Doñana National Park hasn't experienced a single wildfire in nine years. The secret lies not in sophisticated technology, but in a team of patient, four-legged workers.

Donkeys named Leonor, Ainoa, and Ume graze freely across 130 to 150-foot firebreaks, converting dried shrubs and grasses into food. Working seven hours daily from March through November, these animals accomplish what industrial solutions cannot: they gently maintain the landscape while protecting sensitive habitats for Iberian lynx and migratory birds.

Why donkeys over other grazing animals? As Joan Cedó, who launched Catalonia's Tivissa Donkeys Firefighters in 2020, explains, a donkey weighs three times as much as a goat and eats roughly ten times more—meaning its daily impact on vegetation is substantially greater.

The success has inspired neighboring regions. Galicia, Catalonia, Ourense, Navarre, and the Basque Country have all adopted donkey brigades following Andalusia's pioneering work. Cedó reports that since introducing donkeys to his municipality, there have been no wildfires.

This revival of an ancient partnership reflects a growing understanding: the absence of traditional grazing animals has left Spain's forests dangerously overgrown. By reintroducing donkeys—laborers Spain's landscape has relied upon for millennia—communities are rediscovering both ecological wisdom and fire safety.
